title: The Bronze Age collapse
summary: Between about 1200 and 1150 BCE the interconnected palace civilisations of the eastern Mediterranean unravelled with startling speed. The Hittite empire fell, its capital Hattusa burned; the Mycenaean palaces of Pylos, Mycenae, and Tiryns were destroyed; Cypriot cities were sacked; Ugarit on the Syrian coast disappeared in flame; and the Egyptian pharaoh Ramesses III fought a great naval battle in the Nile delta against a coalition of migrating peoples his scribes called simply 'the peoples of the sea'. The year 1177 BCE, made famous by Eric Cline as convenient shorthand, marks the middle of a cascade whose causes are still debated and whose consequences reshaped the ancient world.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
By the thirteenth century BCE the eastern Mediterranean was knit together as no region on earth had ever been before. Great territorial states, the Egyptian New Kingdom under the Ramesside pharaohs, the Hittite empire ruling Anatolia and northern Syria from Hattusa, the Middle Assyrian kingdom on the upper Tigris, Kassite Babylonia on the lower Euphrates, and the Mycenaean palace kingdoms of southern Greece, exchanged embassies, presents, princesses, and grain shipments. The Amarna Letters of the fourteenth century BCE and the Ugaritic archives of the thirteenth make the diplomatic system visible in remarkable detail: a shared vocabulary of brotherhood and gift-giving, an insurance network in which one region's surplus fed another's shortfall, a common trading language (Akkadian) written in a common script (cuneiform).

Beneath the diplomatic surface, the palace economies of the age were unusually fragile. Wealth was concentrated in a handful of monumental centres. Bronze itself, the defining metal, required tin from sources as distant as Cornwall or Central Asia and copper from Cyprus, Sardinia, and the Sinai; disruption of any leg of this trade could paralyse an army. Grain was stockpiled centrally against famine, but the storage systems could handle a bad year, not a bad decade.

By the last quarter of the thirteenth century BCE the strains were showing. Merneptah, son of Ramesses II, boasted in an inscription of the fifth year of his reign (about 1207 BCE) of defeating a coalition of Libyans and unnamed 'northern peoples of all lands', identified with names such as the Ekwesh, Teresh, Lukka, Sherden, and Shekelesh, who had joined a Libyan invasion of the western Delta. The same stele carries the earliest known mention of Israel. Hittite correspondence from the reign of Tudhaliya IV records grain shipments dispatched to Ugarit and Cilicia to relieve famine. Rainfall proxies from Sea of Galilee sediment cores and from stable-isotope studies of Cypriot organic remains show a marked shift toward drier and cooler conditions across the eastern Mediterranean in exactly this period. The whole system was becoming brittle.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: The Lion Gate at Hattusa, capital of the Hittite Empire, which was burned around 1200 BCE.

sections.body:
The collapse itself unfolded over roughly fifty years, from about 1200 to 1150 BCE, but its most concentrated phase falls around 1180 to 1175 BCE. Eric Cline's 2014 book '1177 B.C.: The Year Civilization Collapsed' took its title from the date of Ramesses III's great sea battle in the Nile delta, but Cline himself is careful to insist that the date is a symbol, not a calendar entry.

In Anatolia the Hittite empire disintegrated. The last securely dated correspondence of the Hittite king Suppiluliuma II can be placed around 1207 BCE; a generation later the capital Hattusa, its great walls of gate-lions and Warrior Gods still visible today above the modern town of Bogazkale, was burned to the ground. Whether by external attack, internal revolt, or deliberate evacuation is disputed; recent archaeological work suggests that the palace and royal quarter were emptied of valuables before the fire, implying a controlled abandonment rather than a surprise sack. Either way, the Hittite state ceased to exist. Its provincial capitals in Syria, notably Karkemish, survived as smaller successor kingdoms.

On the Syrian coast Ugarit, one of the richest commercial cities of the Late Bronze Age, was destroyed in a single episode of intense fire, dated by pottery and by the very last cuneiform tablets to just after 1190 BCE. Among the tablets found still baking in the ovens of the royal palace was a letter from Ammurapi, the last king of Ugarit, to the king of Alashiya (Cyprus), pleading for help: 'My father, behold, the enemy's ships came here; my cities were burned, and they did evil things in my country... All my troops are stationed in the Land of Hatti, and all my ships are stationed in the Land of Lycia, and the country is abandoned to itself.' The reply, if there ever was one, does not survive; Ugarit was not rebuilt.

On Cyprus the great copper-producing sites were disrupted, though the scale of destruction is now known to be smaller than earlier scholarship claimed. In the southern Levant a wave of destructions is visible at Hazor, Lachish, Ashkelon, and elsewhere, though again with much local variation and considerable debate about causation.

In Egypt, in the eighth year of Ramesses III (1178 BCE on the conventional chronology), a coalition Egyptian scribes lumped together as the 'peoples of the sea' attacked the Nile delta by land and by sea. Ramesses III commemorated the campaign in a great series of reliefs and inscriptions at his mortuary temple of Medinet Habu near Thebes, which give the earliest surviving depiction of a naval battle. Egyptian archers on the shore and on shipboard poured arrows into the Sea Peoples' vessels, whose distinctive feathered helmets and horned prows are shown being captured or overturned. The named groups, the Peleset, the Tjeker, the Shekelesh, the Denyen, the Weshesh, echo the earlier list of Merneptah; the Peleset are almost certainly the Philistines who then settled on the southern Levantine coast. The battle was an Egyptian victory, but a costly one; Egypt withdrew from its Levantine possessions and would never recover its Late Bronze Age reach.

In the Aegean the Mycenaean palace world came apart. Pylos in the southwestern Peloponnese was destroyed by intense fire around 1180 BCE; the last Linear B tablets, describing frantic preparations against a threat, were baked hard by the flames that consumed the archive. Mycenae and Tiryns in the Argolid were repeatedly damaged. Within two generations the palace-based script and administration had vanished from Greece, and the population dispersed into smaller, poorer villages. What followed is often called the Greek Dark Age, though 'dark' means only that there is no writing; life certainly continued.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: The Medinet Habu relief of Ramesses III's naval battle against the Sea Peoples, c. 1178 BCE.
image.credit: Line drawing after Ramesses III temple reliefs, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
The eastern Mediterranean of 1100 BCE looked profoundly different from that of 1250 BCE. Two of the four great Bronze Age powers, the Hittite empire and the Mycenaean palace system, had ceased to exist. Egypt survived under Ramesses III's successors, but the Twenty-first Dynasty ruled from Tanis in the delta a smaller, poorer state that had lost its Levantine empire. Assyria came through the collapse largely intact and would in the next few centuries expand into the vacuum. Babylonia weakened but continued. In Greece, the palaces did not return; when writing reappeared, some three or four centuries later, it was in a new alphabetic script borrowed from the Phoenicians.

Onto the vacated ground of the old empires new peoples moved. The Phrygians and eventually the Lydians settled central and western Anatolia. Neo-Hittite successor kingdoms preserved fragments of the old Anatolian culture in northern Syria for another five centuries, until they were absorbed by the Neo-Assyrian empire. In the southern Levant a distinctive Iron Age culture emerged, with a Philistine coastal presence, small Aramean kingdoms in the interior, and, from about 1200 BCE onward, a hill-country population that would consolidate into the kingdoms of Israel and Judah. In Greece the Ionians, Aeolians, and Dorians reoccupied and eventually resettled the old palace sites.

The iron industry, whose earliest utilitarian tools appear in Cyprus and the Levant in the twelfth and eleventh centuries BCE, benefited from the collapse of long-distance tin networks that had made bronze possible. By the tenth century BCE iron was the standard metal for weapons and tools across the region: the Iron Age had, quite literally, begun.

Causation remains contested. The Sea Peoples themselves are best understood as a symptom rather than a cause, migrating populations dislodged by problems further upstream. Modern reconstructions typically invoke a 'perfect storm' of environmental, political, and military shocks: severe multi-year drought attested in isotopic and pollen records; a cluster of earthquakes (the 'earthquake storm' proposed by Amos Nur) along the Aegean and Anatolian faults; internal revolts against fragile palace hierarchies; and the collapse of the trade networks on which the whole system depended. The Assyriologist Jesse Millek has recently argued that some of the 'destructions' long taken as canonical have been over-counted or misdated, suggesting the collapse was messier and more regionally variable than the classic narrative suggests.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Bronze Age collapse has become, in modern historical writing, the paradigm case of the sudden failure of a complex interconnected system. Cline's 1177 B.C., translated into more than a dozen languages, has been read as a warning to a modern globalised economy about the risks of cascading failure. Historians of climate, of trade networks, and of state collapse routinely draw on it as a case study.

For centuries after 1150 BCE, the memory of the lost Late Bronze Age worked itself into legend. The Iliad and Odyssey, composed in their surviving form around the eighth century BCE but drawing on much older oral traditions, remember a heroic age of chariot-riding kings and walled cities that corresponds recognisably to the Mycenaean palace world; Troy, whose ruins at Hisarlik were sacked in the thirteenth century BCE, became the setting of the founding epic of Greek literature. The Hebrew Bible's stories of a Philistine coastal enemy, of Aramean rivals in the north, and of the migration of Israel from Egypt to the hill country of Canaan, are set in exactly the period of the collapse and its aftermath.

Modern archaeology continues to add detail. Excavations at Hattusa (since 1906 by German teams, most recently under Andreas Schachner), at Ugarit (since 1929 by the French mission at Ras Shamra), at Medinet Habu (recorded by the Chicago Epigraphic Survey since the 1920s), and at Pylos (since 1939 by Carl Blegen and now by University of Cincinnati teams) have collectively transformed our sense of the age. The lesson they teach is a sobering one: even richly connected, technologically sophisticated, apparently robust civilisations can, when enough things go wrong at once, simply stop.

worldContext.text: In the northwestern Indian subcontinent the Late Harappan phase was in its final centuries; the remnants of the great planned cities had contracted into smaller village societies. Indo-Aryan speakers, whose ancestors had moved out of Central Asia several centuries earlier, were consolidating in the Punjab. The oldest hymns of the Rigveda, whose language is closely related to Old Iranian and to reconstructed Proto-Indo-European, were probably being composed in this era, though their fixation in the form we have took another millennium.

In China the late Shang dynasty, ruling from the capital at Yin (modern Anyang), was in its middle period. The reign of Wu Ding (r. c. 1250 to 1192 BCE) had been the political and cultural peak of the dynasty; his successors continued to cast the great bronze ritual vessels and to consult the ancestors by scapulomancy on turtle plastrons and ox scapulae. The rising Zhou state on the western frontier, which in 1046 BCE would overthrow the last Shang king Di Xin at the Battle of Muye, was quietly gathering strength.

In Egypt, the Twentieth Dynasty of Ramesses III fought off the Sea Peoples but at heavy cost. The reign ended with a harem conspiracy against the aging pharaoh, documented in the extraordinary Judicial Papyrus of Turin. Successor pharaohs presided over declining wealth, tomb robberies at Thebes, and the slow slippage of central authority. South of Egypt, in Nubia, the Egyptian occupation of the Kushite territory persisted for another century before withdrawal; local kingdoms would in time re-emerge and, by the eighth century BCE, conquer Egypt itself as the Twenty-fifth Dynasty.

On the Gulf coast of what is now Veracruz, Mexico, the Olmec ceremonial city of San Lorenzo was at or near its peak, its plateau crowned with colossal basalt heads probably representing rulers. In the Andean highlands of Peru small ceremonial sites were consolidating into what would become the Chavin tradition of the first millennium BCE. Elsewhere in the Americas, from the Mississippi valley to the Amazon basin, complex tribal and chiefdom societies flourished, but there is no evidence of writing, metal tools, or state-scale political organisation.

In the western Pacific the Lapita cultural complex, marked by its distinctive dentate-stamped pottery, had spread from the Bismarck Archipelago through the Solomons to Vanuatu, New Caledonia, Fiji, and by around 1000 BCE to Tonga and Samoa. This was the first colonisation of Remote Oceania by human beings, and it was accomplished by Austronesian voyagers in outrigger canoes. Australia continued to be inhabited by its long-established populations of hunter-gatherer peoples.

figures.name: Ramesses III
figures.role: Second pharaoh of Egypt's Twentieth Dynasty (r. 1186 to 1155 BCE), whose victory over the Sea Peoples in the delta in 1178 BCE is recorded at Medinet Habu

figures.name: Ammurapi
figures.role: Last king of Ugarit, whose desperate letter appealing to Cyprus for naval assistance was found baking in the ovens of his destroyed palace

figures.name: Suppiluliuma II
figures.role: Last known Hittite Great King, whose reign ended with the destruction and abandonment of the capital Hattusa

figures.name: Merneptah
figures.role: Egyptian pharaoh (r. c. 1213 to 1203 BCE) whose Great Karnak Inscription records the first named appearance of both the Sea Peoples and Israel

figures.name: Eric Cline
figures.role: American historian and archaeologist whose 2014 book '1177 B.C.: The Year Civilization Collapsed' fixed a symbolic date for the whole cascade in modern popular memory

images.caption: Nineteenth-century line drawing of the Medinet Habu relief showing Ramesses III's naval battle against the Sea Peoples in the eighth year of his reign, c. 1178 BCE.
images.credit: Unknown artist in the pay of Ramesses III, via Wikimedia Commons
